content:
Bug:
Config file had this, unchanged for months:
"command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/hooks/session.js start" Environment variable inside a shell command inside a JSON string. When the runtime reads the JSON and invokes the command through a shell, the shell expands $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR and word-splits it. If the expansion contains whitespace, argv breaks.
Old bug, classic Bash 101, fix is to wrap the variable in double quotes and move on. The interesting part is not the fix. It is why I did not catch it for months.
How it manifested:
The runtime is Claude Code (the AI coding CLI). It sets $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR to the project root and invokes the hook. My project root is /Users/saravanan/claude context graph/some-project/. Two spaces. Every hook invocation for months was failing with:
Error: Cannot find module '/Users/saravanan/claude' Node.js v23.11.0 Note the module name. Not a full path. First whitespace-delimited token of my project path. The shell split CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR into three argv slots, Node saw the first slot, tried to load it as a module, failed. The error message references a path fragment that no user searching their machine for troubleshooting would ever recognize as their own project path.
Why my tests missed it:
600-ish tests. All green. Zero coverage of this specific failure mode.
Reasons:

• Tests spawn hooks via subprocess.run(["node", path]), which bypasses the shell. Word-splitting is a shell-lifetime behavior. No shell in the test path means the bug cannot occur.

• Test paths are /tmp/pytest-*. No spaces in /tmp. Even if a test had shelled out, it would have hit the happy case.

• The runtime failure was silent from my program's perspective. The hook exited nonzero, the runtime logged the failure into a per-session transcript at ~/.claude/projects/*.jsonl, and my program above the hook layer never got called. Nothing in the layers I was watching noticed anything wrong.
Green tests plus green CI plus silent runtime failure equals no signal.
How I actually found it:
Ran my tool's state-capture on my own repository. Looked at the row counts across the tables it writes to. Some tables were populated. Some tables that should have been growing month over month were sitting at zero. Absolute count looked fine (about 1200 rows total). Ratio between the tables that write on hook invocations versus the tables that write from other sources was wrong.
Dug into the session transcripts, found the hook error, worked backward from the module name to the path fragment to the space in my directory to the missing quotes.
Lessons I actually care about:
Silent partial failure is worse than loud total failure. Total failure trips monitors. Partial failure passes every check that measures "is anything getting through" because something is, just not everything. The observability shape needed to catch this class of bug is not "are the writes reaching my datastore," it is "is the write rate proportional to the invocation rate of the upstream event." Very few systems build that upfront.
Test paths without spaces are a common shape of test-vs-reality gap. /tmp/pytest-* is convenient and reproducible. It does not reflect the paths real users have. macOS defaults put user work under ~/Documents/. Corporate paths have Work Stuff and Client X in them. Any test that generates shell commands and executes them from a synthetic path is missing the class of bug that lives in shell interpretation of user path characters. The regression test I ended up shipping constructs a path with a space in it, sets $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR to that path, and asserts the generated command string quotes the variable. Should have been there from day one.
Fix itself is trivial. Wrap the variable in quotes. Add the regression test.
The failure mode is the writeup, not the fix. Unquoted expansion, plus subprocess-based tests that skip the shell layer, plus test paths without spaces, plus silent partial failure downstream. Any one of those alone is manageable. All four together produced months of shipped brokenness that nothing complained about.

  submitted by   /u/Funky_Chicken_22
[link]   [comments]
